svg.text: Why semantic similarity isn't enough, and how to fix it with two-stage retrieval

g.text: What is the capital of Canada?
The capital of France is in Paris
Canada is the maple syrup capital of the world
Ottawa is the capital of Canada
All four contain "capital" or "Canada" — but only ONE actually answers the question.

svg.text: ● "Toronto is in Canada" — close in vector space (both about Canadian geography)
● "maple syrup capital" — keyword bait, wrong sense of "capital"
● The correct answer "Ottawa" exists but isn't ranked first
2. The fix — a two-stage retrieval pipeline
3. Before vs. after — same docs, different order

g.text: 2. The capital of France is in Paris
3. Canada is the maple syrup capital of the world
4. Ottawa is the capital of Canada

svg.text: The actual answer is buried at position #4

g.text: 1. Ottawa is the capital of Canada
3. The capital of France is in Paris
4. Canada is the maple syrup capital of the world

svg.text: Ottawa shoots to #1 → the LLM gets the right context first

g.text: Run cross-encoder only on the top 20–100 docs
from initial retrieval → quality-time tradeoff feasible.
Too slow to run on millions of docs directly.
Powerful but costly → too slow for large-scale retrieval.
Best used for reranking AFTER initial filtering.
In 2026 also done by prompting general LLMs (RankGPT-style).
✓ Initial retrieval optimizes recall: get the answer
✓ Reranking optimizes precision: put it at #1.
✓ You still return only 5–10 docs — but they're
far more relevant → better LLM answers.
● Cohere Rerank 3 • the de-facto API choice (multilingual)
● Voyage rerank-2 • strong on RAG-style queries
● Jina Reranker v2 • fast, multilingual, free tier

svg.text: Adding a reranker is one of the highest-ROI upgrades you can make to a basic RAG pipeline — usually a 10–25% quality lift for ~50 ms extra latency.
